title: Mislabeled Bags

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-02-brain-teasers.md

setup.narrative: There are three bags of fruit:
rules: One bag contains a MIX of apples and oranges.
All three bags carry labels (APPLES / ORANGES / MIX), but EVERY bag is mislabeled.
You may pick any number of fruits, one at a time, from any bag(s).
setup.question: What is the minimum number of fruit picks needed to identify all three bags correctly?

learningObjectives: See how the 'all wrong' constraint creates a cyclic permutation symmetry that limits the possible truths.
Pick the bag whose mislabeling collapses to the most informative case.
Chain a single observation into three forced identifications.

stages.label: Comprehension of the constraint
stages.prompt: If the bag labeled 'MIX' is mislabeled, what could it actually contain?

options.text: Apples only OR oranges only -- it CANNOT contain a mix.
Anything -- mislabeling means we know nothing.
Mix only -- the label is just wrong about the order.

stages.explanation: Mislabeled means the label is WRONG. If the label says MIX, the contents are NOT a mix -- they must be one of the two pure options.

stages.label: Choose your technique

options.text: Symmetry -- pick from the bag whose mislabeling forces the strongest deduction. The 'MIX' bag's mislabeling forces it to be PURE -- one fruit reveals everything.
Pick from each bag once for safety (3 picks).
Probability over which bag is which.

stages.explanation: The 'all mislabeled' constraint creates a cyclic-permutation symmetry: there are exactly 2 valid configurations (the labels rotate one way or the other). A single observation eliminates one of the two -- if you sample the right bag.

stages.label: Why pick from MIX?
stages.prompt: Why pick from the bag labeled MIX rather than from one of the others?

options.text: Because that bag is FORCED to be pure (since its label is wrong) -- so a single fruit DETERMINES its true contents.
Because mix bags are hardest to identify, we want to spend our pick there.
It doesn't matter -- any pick works.

stages.explanation: The bag labeled APPLES could be MIX or ORANGES -- a fruit you draw might come from a mix and not pin it down. The bag labeled MIX, by contrast, is mislabeled and therefore PURE -- one fruit settles it. Then the cyclic symmetry handles the other two.

stages.label: Apply the cascade
stages.prompt: You pick from the MIX-labeled bag and draw an APPLE. What are the actual contents of each bag?

options.text: MIX-label = APPLES, APPLES-label = ORANGES, ORANGES-label = MIX
MIX-label = APPLES, APPLES-label = MIX,     ORANGES-label = ORANGES
MIX-label = MIX,    APPLES-label = ORANGES, ORANGES-label = APPLES
MIX-label = APPLES, APPLES-label = APPLES,  ORANGES-label = MIX

stages.explanation: Reasoning chain: (1) MIX-label drew an apple and was mislabeled -- so it's pure APPLES. (2) ORANGES-label can't hold ORANGES (mislabeled) and can't hold APPLES (taken) -- so it holds MIX. (3) APPLES-label can't hold APPLES (mislabeled) and the only one left is ORANGES. Three bags identified after one pick. Options (b)-(d) violate at least one mislabel constraint.

stages.label: Commit
stages.prompt: What is the minimum number of picks?
stages.explanation: 1 pick from the MIX-label bag is sufficient. The cyclic symmetry of 'all wrong' lets a single observation cascade through to identify all three.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: What if only TWO of the three bags were mislabeled (and one was correctly labeled)? Could you still solve it in one pick?
Generalize to N bags with N-1 pure types and 1 mix, all mislabeled. How many picks?
What's the cyclic permutation that 'all mislabeled' creates?
